Within the first three months post MDA, MTP dropped from 340 to 95 (72% reduction).

The dynamics in the infection and infective rates and the monthly transmission potential of O. volvulus infective larvae from flies to humans at different time points before and after IVM-MDA are illustrated below.
71.Rationale: The passage provides data on the entomological indicators, such as infection and infective rates, and the monthly transmission potential (MTP) of O. volvulus before and after IVM-MDA. Despite high geographical and therapeutic coverage of ivermectin mass drug administration, the infection and infective rates remained above 19 per 1,000 parous flies at all collection time points, and the MTP increased after 30 days of treatment, indicating that the transmission of O. volvulus is not fully controlled. This suggests that factors such as non-compliance with treatment or the presence of parasite strains with sub-optimal response to ivermectin are maintaining the transmission of onchocerciasis in the Mbam drainage basin.

71.Option: The construction of the Mape and Bamendjing dams has led to a permanent increase in blackfly breeding sites, making onchocerciasis elimination impossible in the Mbam drainage basin without large-scale dam removal.
Evidence: The Mbam drainage basin is irrigated by many fast-flowing rivers including the Mbam and the Noun rivers (Fig 6). The source of these rivers is in the highlands far from the Bafia Health District.
The Mbam River has its origin at around 1,900m above sea level in the mountains forming the border with Nigeria to the North-North-East of Banyo in the Adamaoua Region of Cameroon.
The Mbam River descends very quickly from North to South to East of Bafoussam. While the slope decreases, the river’s direction bends towards the South-East, just before the town of Bafia.
The Mbam River forms a very large bend radius, receives river Ndjim on its left bank and merges with Sanaga at an altitude of 380m after a course of 400 km.

The Mape reservoir-dam, located at 11 km Northeast of Magba, on the Mape, was built in 1988 in the district of Bankim (Adamawa region, Mayo-Banyo division) and it is located at latitude 6 °01’56” north and longitude 11°18’18” east.

The Mape reservoir-dam, located at 11 km Northeast of Magba, on the Mape, was built in 1988 in the district of Bankim (Adamawa region, Mayo-Banyo division) and it is located at latitude 6 °01’56” north and longitude 11°18’18” east.
71.Rationale: The passage does not provide any evidence to support the claim that the construction of the Mape and Bamendjing dams has led to a permanent increase in blackfly breeding sites, making onchocerciasis elimination impossible in the Mbam drainage basin without large-scale dam removal. The passage only provides information about the location and characteristics of the dams, but does not discuss their impact on blackfly breeding or on the elimination of onchocerciasis.

71.Option: The high monthly transmission potential (MTP) observed at 6 and 9 months post-MDA indicates that ivermectin treatment is ineffective in reducing microfilarial load in the human population, leading to sustained transmission by vectors.
Evidence: The monthly transmission potential (MTP) of O. volvulus infective larvae from flies to humans dropped from 340 L3H/man/month before IVM-MDA to 229 L3H/man/month after 30 days of treatment. The MTP further decreased to 95 L3H/man/month at D90 but drastically increased to 205 and 471 L3H/man/month at six and nine months post treatment, respectively (Fig 4). Within the first three months post MDA, MTP dropped from 340 to 95 (72% reduction).
The infection and infective rates were above 19 per 1,000 parous flies at all collection time points pre- and post-treatment and in the rainy and dry seasons.
The average infection rate in this area was 41.2 per 1,000 parous flies (Table 1).

The infective rate was also analysed over all observed time points (Fig 3). Before IVM-MDA, the infective rate was 14.6 per 1,000 parous flies and increased to 15.4 per 1,000 parous flies at 30 days post MDA but later decreased at 90 and 180 days after treatment. By day 270 post IVM-MDA, the infective rate had increased to 12.7 per 1,000 parous flies. On average the infectivity rate in this study was 11 per thousand parous flies (Table 1).
71.Rationale: The passage provides data on the entomological indicators, such as infection and infective rates, and the monthly transmission potential (MTP) of O. volvulus before and after IVM-MDA. Despite high geographical and therapeutic coverage of ivermectin mass drug administration, the infection and infective rates remained above 19 per 1,000 parous flies at all collection time points, and the MTP increased after 30 days of treatment, indicating that the transmission of O. volvulus is not fully controlled. This suggests that factors such as non-compliance with treatment or the presence of parasite strains with sub-optimal response to ivermectin are maintaining the transmission of onchocerciasis in the Mbam drainage basin. The high MTP observed at 6 and 9 months post-MDA does not necessarily indicate that ivermectin treatment is ineffective in reducing microfilarial load in the human population, as other factors such as non-compliance with treatment or the presence of parasite strains with sub-optimal response to ivermectin may be contributing to the sustained transmission.

71.Option: The detection of O. ochengi in two body pools at 9 months post-MDA suggests that animal Onchocerca species are a significant source of transmission in the Mbam drainage basin, contributing to the persistence of onchocerciasis despite ivermectin treatment.
Evidence: Using the triplex real-time PCR for the detection of O. ochengi parasites (larvae) in the Simulium damnosum, none of the 129 head pools were positive (0%) while two of the 129 body pools (1.6%) had positive signals. This gave us a zero infective rate and an infection rate of 1.4 (n = 129 pools of 100 flies with 127 negative pools) per 1,000 parous flies. The two pools that were positive for O. ochengi were collected at 9 months post MDA in April 2017.
If there is the possibility that other Onchocerca species are also present, molecular techniques that differentiate O. volvulus from O. ochengi or O. ramachandani should be employed.
71.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the detection of O. ochengi in two body pools at 9 months post-MDA suggests that animal Onchocerca species are a significant source of transmission in the Mbam drainage basin, contributing to the persistence of onchocerciasis despite ivermectin treatment. The presence of O. ochengi in the blackfly population indicates that this animal species may be a reservoir for the parasite and contribute to the ongoing transmission of onchocerciasis in the area.

72.Option: Healthcare workers who perform skin biopsies on patients with cutaneous leishmaniasis are at risk of occupational infection through accidental needlestick injuries, and clinicians should consider this route of transmission when evaluating unexplained skin lesions in such individuals.
Evidence: The resident suffered an accidental needlestick injury on the left index finger.
The Haitian patient was diagnosed with mucocutaneous leishmaniasis caused by Leishmania panamensis.
A skin biopsy was compatible with leishmaniasis.
72.Rationale: The passage describes an incident where a healthcare worker (the resident) suffered an accidental needlestick injury while performing a skin biopsy on a patient with cutaneous leishmaniasis. The resident subsequently developed symptoms consistent with leishmaniasis, indicating that the needlestick injury was the route of transmission. This case supports the inference that healthcare workers are at risk of occupational infection through such injuries and that clinicians should consider this route when evaluating unexplained skin lesions in patients with potential vector-borne parasitic infections like leishmaniasis.

72.Option: The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention has guidelines for preventing the transmission of infectious diseases in the healthcare workplace, including needlestick and other sharps-related injury prevention programs, which should be strictly followed to prevent occupational leishmaniasis.
Evidence: The passage does not mention the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) or any specific guidelines they have for preventing the transmission of infectious diseases in the healthcare workplace.
72.Rationale: The passage does not provide any information about the CDC or its guidelines for preventing the transmission of infectious diseases in the healthcare workplace. Therefore, this option cannot be inferred from the passage body.

72.Option: The successful treatment of the resident with meglumine antimoniate and intralesional injections indicates that cutaneous leishmaniasis following occupational exposure is inherently less severe than the mucocutaneous form seen in the patient.
Evidence: The passage does not provide a direct comparison of the severity of cutaneous leishmaniasis following occupational exposure to the mucocutaneous form seen in the patient.
72.Rationale: The passage only describes the successful treatment of the resident with leishmaniasis. It does not provide any information to infer that cutaneous leishmaniasis following occupational exposure is inherently less severe than the mucocutaneous form. Therefore, this option cannot be derived from the passage.

72.Option: The delayed onset of symptoms two months after the needlestick injury suggests that the resident’s immune system was unable to control the Leishmania infection initially, leading to a prolonged incubation period and eventual clinical manifestation.
Evidence: The passage does not provide any information about the resident's immune system or the reasons for the delayed onset of symptoms.
72.Rationale: The passage does not provide any information about the resident's immune system or the reasons for the delayed onset of symptoms. Therefore, this option cannot be derived from the passage.

73.Option: The observed clinical signs and onset of disease are alike with what has been described for HPS in humans and the ANDV model in Syrian hamsters, suggesting that the immune system plays a central role in disease development through overactivation.
Evidence: HPS onset is characterized by typical viral symptoms: headache, fever, myalgia, nausea, diarrhea and even abdominal pain are frequently present.
Infection of immunosuppressed hamsters with New World hantaviruses results in high mortality
Infection of immunosuppressed hamsters with BAYV and BCCV mimics HPS
Passive immunization was carried out to confirm that the virus was responsible for the observed disease and mortality.
73.Rationale: The passage describes the clinical signs and onset of disease in HPS and the ANDV model in Syrian hamsters, which are similar to those observed in humans with HPS. The high mortality rate in immunosuppressed hamsters and the prevention of mortality with passive immunization suggest that the immune system's overactivation plays a central role in disease development. This inference is supported by the evidence that immunosuppression leads to disease and that neutralizing antibodies can prevent disease, indicating the immune system's involvement in disease progression.

73.Option: The passive immunization experiment confirms that the immune system is responsible for the observed disease pathology, as neutralizing antibodies prevented mortality in infected hamsters.
Evidence: Passive immunization was carried out to confirm that the virus was responsible for the observed disease and mortality.
In the groups challenged with BCCV and BAYV all animals died within 21 days after infection.
In the test groups, receiving the BAYV and BCCV antibodies, all 8 hamsters in each group were still alive at the end of the experiment on day 35 post infection.
73.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that passive immunization with neutralizing antibodies prevented mortality in hamsters infected with hantaviruses, confirming that the immune system is responsible for the observed disease pathology. This is a direct inference from the experimental results showing that immunized hamsters survived while non-immunized ones did not.

73.Option: The fact that immunosuppressive agents are able to make Syrian hamsters vulnerable for hantaviruses other than Andes virus indicates that an interesting and probably complicated interplay between the virus and the immune system is responsible for the occurrence of hantavirus disease and its associated mortality.
Evidence: Infection of immunosuppressed hamsters with New World hantaviruses results in high mortality
Infection of immunosuppressed hamsters with BAYV and BCCV mimics HPS
Passive immunization was carried out to confirm that the virus was responsible for the observed disease and mortality.
73.Rationale: The passage indicates that immunosuppressive agents make hamsters vulnerable to hantaviruses, leading to high mortality. This suggests a complex interplay between the virus and the immune system, as the immune system's suppression leads to increased susceptibility to the virus and associated mortality. The evidence from the passage supports this inference.

73.Option: The lack of mortality in immunocompetent Syrian hamsters infected with New World hantaviruses other than Andes virus suggests that viral pathogenicity is primarily determined by host-specific immune responses rather than viral genetic factors.
Evidence: Infection of immunosuppressed hamsters with New World hantaviruses results in high mortality
Infection of immunosuppressed hamsters with BAYV and BCCV mimics HPS
Passive immunization was carried out to confirm that the virus was responsible for the observed disease and mortality.
73.Rationale: The passage does not provide information about the mortality rates in immunocompetent Syrian hamsters infected with New World hantaviruses other than Andes virus. Therefore, this inference cannot be drawn from the passage. The passage only discusses the effects of immunosuppression on disease outcome, not the specific role of host immunity in the absence of immunosuppression.

73.Option: The use of immunosuppressive agents enables the induction of lethal disease in Syrian hamsters infected with multiple New World hantaviruses, indicating that the immune system plays a critical role in modulating disease severity and that viral pathogenicity is not solely determined by the virus itself.
Evidence: Infection of immunosuppressed hamsters with New World hantaviruses results in high mortality
Infection of immunosuppressed hamsters with BAYV and BCCV mimics HPS
Passive immunization was carried out to confirm that the virus was responsible for the observed disease and mortality.
73.Rationale: The passage indicates that the use of immunosuppressive agents leads to high mortality in hamsters infected with New World hantaviruses, suggesting that the immune system plays a critical role in modulating disease severity. This inference is supported by the evidence that immunosuppression leads to lethal disease, indicating that the immune system's role is significant in determining the outcome of the infection.

74.Option: The variation in infection prevalence between villages over time is best explained by differences in treatment coverage and local socioeconomic factors.
Evidence: The mean coverage of antibiotic at a village visit was 94.1% relative to the census, ranging from 73.9% to 100% of those eligible for treatment (Table 1).
In the baseline census, the 16 villages contained 5735 individuals in 1348 households. There were 808 1–5 year-old children, 48.6% of whom were girls (95% CI 45.3 to 51.9%).
74.Rationale: The passage provides information on treatment coverage and demographic factors, but it does not offer a direct explanation for the variation in infection prevalence between villages over time. The evidence given does not establish a causal link between the variation in infection prevalence and the differences in treatment coverage or local socioeconomic factors. Therefore, this inference cannot be derived solely from the passage body.

74.Option: The return of infection after treatment discontinuation suggests that the antibiotic resistance is developing in the chlamydial population.
Evidence: Infection increased in the 24 months after the last treatment in 14 of 16 villages, but remained lower than it had been before the first treatment in all 16 villages.
The mean prevalence of infection (black) and the prevalence in each of the individual 16 study villages (grey) are plotted against time.
74.Rationale: The passage does not provide any direct evidence of antibiotic resistance developing in the chlamydial population. The return of infection after treatment discontinuation could be due to various factors, such as incomplete treatment coverage, reinfection, or other epidemiological dynamics. The passage does not mention any changes in the sensitivity of the chlamydial population to azithromycin, which would be necessary to support the inference of developing antibiotic resistance.

74.Option: Infection may never return if it is brought to a low enough level, analogous to the Allee effect found in population biology.
Evidence: Infection increased in the 24 months after the last treatment in 14 of 16 villages, but remained lower than it had been before the first treatment in all 16 villages.
The mean prevalence of infection (black) and the prevalence in each of the individual 16 study villages (grey) are plotted against time.
74.Rationale: The passage does not provide any evidence to support the analogy with the Allee effect, which describes a situation where a population's growth rate decreases as the population density decreases. The passage does not discuss the population dynamics of the chlamydial infection or the concept of a critical population density below which the infection may not return. Therefore, this inference cannot be derived from the passage body.

74.Option: Even after four biannual distributions of azithromycin, infection predictably returns in trachoma-endemic communities, indicating that mass antibiotic distributions cannot be discontinued without risk of resurgence.
Evidence: Infection increased in the 24 months after the last treatment in 14 of 16 villages, but remained lower than it had been before the first treatment in all 16 villages.

The mean prevalence of infection (black) and the prevalence in each of the individual 16 study villages (grey) are plotted against time. Points represent estimates of the prevalence of infection in 1–5 year-old children at a single village-visit. Lines connecting points were constructed for illustrative purposes in three ways. Before the first treatment, infection was presumed to be constant. After each treatment (black arrow), infection was presumed to decrease by the estimated coverage level, before returning in the subsequent 6 months to the observed prevalence. After the last treatment, infection was presumed to proceed linearly between each time point.
74.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that infection increased in the 24 months after the last treatment in 14 of 16 villages, but remained lower than it had been before the first treatment in all 16 villages. This pattern suggests that infection is likely to return after treatment discontinuation, indicating that mass antibiotic distributions cannot be discontinued without risk of resurgence. The passage provides a clear epidemiological observation that supports this inference.

76.Option: The higher total costs associated P. vivax infections compared to other Plasmodium species may be explained by longer treatment duration, more frequent clinic visits, and higher caregiver involvement due to the younger age profile of affected patients.
Evidence: P. vivax patients presented with the highest total costs at $199.05 (SD=173.87; Table 4).

The majority of direct costs were related to the hospital admission, although it is unclear whether these costs included direct admission costs or cost associated food and other supplies needed during an admission.
Indirect household costs were the most significant contributor to the total cost burden (58%), with a mean total of US$75.84 (SD=69.67) for each household.
76.Rationale: The passage does not provide specific information about the treatment duration, frequency of clinic visits, or caregiver involvement for P. vivax infections compared to other Plasmodium species. The higher total costs for P. vivax are mentioned, but the reasons for this are not detailed in the passage. Therefore, this inference cannot be logically derived from the evidence provided.

76.Option: The use of standardized wage estimates from national surveys to value productivity losses resulted in a 78% increase in total cost per episode, indicating that the base case underestimates the true economic burden of malaria on households.
Evidence: When using Sabah-specific estimates from the Malaysia Household Income survey (US$223.57 per month) applied to only adults in Scenario 2, the total costs increased by 19% to US$158.85.
When this wage was applied to the time loss of all patients in Scenario 3, the total cost increased to US$178.61, an increase of 37% compared to the base case.
76.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that using standardized wage estimates from national surveys to value productivity losses resulted in a significant increase in the total cost per malaria episode. This supports the inference that the base case underestimates the true economic burden of malaria on households.

76.Option: Households in Sabah face a disproportionate financial burden from malaria due to the high prevalence of zoonotic P. knowlesi transmission among working-age males in rural agricultural areas, which increases both direct and indirect costs.
Evidence: P. knowlesi infections occur in areas inhabited by natural macaque hosts where it is transmitted to humans via Anopheles Leucosphyrus Group mosquito vectors.
P. knowlesi is now the major cause of malaria in Malaysia, with 3000 to 4000 malaria case notifications per year since 2017.
P. knowlesi infections carry a high risk of severe disease, with 6-9% of symptomatic cases presenting to health facilities in East Malaysia classified as severe malaria.
The increase in the incidence of P. knowlesi [22] is primarily in the East Malaysia states of Sabah and Sarawak on the island of Borneo.
In 2017 Sabah accounted for 45% of all reported malaria cases in Malaysia, with an almost five times greater annual case-incidence than the national average.
76.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of the high prevalence of P. knowlesi, particularly among working-age males in rural agricultural areas, and the associated high risk of severe disease. This supports the inference that households in Sabah face a disproportionate financial burden from malaria due to the high prevalence of zoonotic P. knowlesi transmission.

76.Option: Severe malaria cases, all due to P. knowlesi infection, were associated with substantially higher indirect costs, primarily due to longer hospital stays and greater productivity losses among working-age males.
Evidence: The seven severe malaria cases (all P. knowlesi) had 91% higher total costs than non-severe cases (US$239.64 versus US$125.27 respectively, p=0.006) (Table 4), predominantly due to substantially higher indirect costs (US$89.25 mean difference) for severe malaria.

The mean length of time from enrolment to performing the interview was 31 days. Characteristics of the study participants are presented in Table 1. The mean age was 32 years old (SD = 18), and 75% (114/152) were male.
The majority of patients were diagnosed with P. knowlesi infections (71%, 108/152). Seven (5%) patients met the WHO research criteria for severe malaria; all were due to P. knowlesi infection.
The increase in the incidence of P. knowlesi [22] is primarily in the East Malaysia states of Sabah and Sarawak on the island of Borneo.
In 2017 Sabah accounted for 45% of all reported malaria cases in Malaysia, with an almost five times greater annual case-incidence than the national average [26].
76.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that severe malaria cases, all due to P. knowlesi infection, were associated with substantially higher indirect costs. This supports the inference that these cases were associated with longer hospital stays and greater productivity losses, particularly among working-age males.

76.Option: Despite the provision of free health-facility-related costs of malaria care by the Ministry of Health, Malaysia, patients in Sabah faced substantial out-of-pocket costs for a single malaria episode, with a mean of $131, and a large proportion of households experienced catastrophic health expenditure, particularly when using lower income thresholds.
Evidence: Direct household costs equated to US$53.89 (SD=66.20) per person (41% of the total cost).
Indirect household costs were the most significant contributor to the total cost burden (58%), with a mean total of US$75.84 (SD=69.67) for each household.
When using the mean monthly self-reported income value of US$148, direct health expenditure for a single malaria episode accounted for 27% of monthly income.
Eighty percent (122/152) of patients reported direct health costs above 10% of monthly income, indicating catastrophic health expenditure.

When using a higher defined threshold for catastrophic health expenditure of 25% of monthly income, the direct costs of a malaria episode reported by 38% (57/152) of patients in this survey remained at levels consistent with catastrophic health expenditure.
76.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that patients in Sabah faced substantial out-of-pocket costs for a single malaria episode, with a mean of $131. It also indicates that a large proportion of households experienced catastrophic health expenditure, particularly when using lower income thresholds. This supports the inference that despite the provision of free health-facility-related costs of malaria care, patients faced significant financial burdens.

77.Option: Negative perceptions of tsetse traps are prone to change over time, particularly after exposure to the technology and through community education, leading to sustained acceptance and active participation in control efforts.
Evidence: Participants expressed willingness and appeared motivated to be involved in tsetse control programs; although the HAT+ group expressed this willingness more frequently.

Some participants from the HAT− group, for instance reported that, on some occasion their first contact with traps was accompanied by the explanation of the trap purpose or general explanation that traps are beneficial. This communication mostly happened through personnel carrying out trap deployment or another member of community, who had been informed about it previously. According to participants this information helped them to put aside associations with supernatural powers.

Similarly participants from HAT+ villages, communicated changed negative- initial attitudes: 'Before we heard of them [traps] we didn't like them; but now we have got to know them [the traps], we like them (…). We like them because since that time they deployed them this has reduced the number of tsetse flies in the area'.

This figure indicates that initial acceptance of tsetse traps when they are initiated in the community is low. This was the case HAT+ villages at the beginning of the tsetse control intervention about ten years ago and is currently still evident in HAT− villages. With the time this acceptance increases and is irreversible once achieved.
77.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that community perceptions and attitudes towards tsetse traps change over time, especially after being exposed to the technology and receiving education about its purpose. This change in perception leads to sustained acceptance and active participation in control efforts. The evidence shows that initial negative reactions give way to positive attitudes as the community becomes more familiar with the traps and understands their role in disease control.

77.Option: The geographical proximity of HAT+ and HAT− villages suggests that information about tsetse control programs spreads easily between communities, leading to similar levels of acceptance over time.
Evidence: Villages from HAT+ and HAT− areas lie within 10 kilometres of each other. Villagers from different villages often mix in the local markets and during social events such as celebrations of country independence, women's day, Christmas; hence the differences in views described below were surprising.
77.Rationale: The passage indicates that despite the geographical proximity of HAT+ and HAT− villages, there are still differences in views and perceptions about tsetse control programs. This suggests that information about tsetse control programs does not spread easily between communities, leading to different levels of acceptance over time. The evidence contradicts the inference that similar levels of acceptance are achieved due to the proximity of the villages.

77.Option: The effectiveness of tsetse traps in reducing disease incidence is the main reason for community acceptance, regardless of prior exposure or cultural beliefs.
Evidence: The majority of participants described two or three different groups of biting insect, which they classify under category of tsetse (Lugbara, wii or ofii). The subcategories included 'the infectious tsetse' and non-'infectious tsetse', the last being mostly recognized as causing biting nuisance to humans and cattle. The 'infectious tsetse' were mostly described as big in size, having sharp mouth parts, lighter in colour compared to other flies, by participants of both groups.

The majority of participants described the purpose of traps for capturing tsetse flies or other insects. In the HAT− group this conclusion was arrived at mostly from observation of trapped insects on in the upper part of the trap. In this group, participants mostly made associations of traps with mosquitoes however some were certain that traps were specifically for killing tsetse flies.

Participants in both groups agreed that traps work effectively, however the HAT+ group provided more specific details. Participants, for instance, used the following observations for describing effectiveness: reduction in tsetse numbers, reduction of tsetse bites, protection of community against tsetse, and reduction of HAT cases in the area.
77.Rationale: The passage suggests that while the effectiveness of tsetse traps in reducing disease incidence is a factor in community acceptance, it is not the only reason. Cultural beliefs and prior exposure to the technology also play a role in shaping community perceptions. The evidence shows that community acceptance is influenced by a combination of factors, not just the effectiveness of the traps.

77.Option: Community willingness to participate in tsetse control is primarily driven by financial incentives, as evidenced by men's expressed interest in government involvement and financial motivation.
Evidence: Participants mostly requested having access to traps and being involved in their deployment and distribution. The HAT+ group reported that they were already contributing to tsetse control by voluntarily maintaining traps, slashing vegetation around watering points, and burning bushes. More women than men reported being involved in these activities. In both groups women expressed more need for empowerment (see a quote below) and requested access to traps more often, while men expressed more need for village decision making, financial motivation and government involvement in tsetse control programs.
77.Rationale: The passage suggests that community willingness to participate in tsetse control is driven by a combination of factors, including access to traps, involvement in their deployment, and empowerment. While financial incentives and government involvement are mentioned, they are not presented as the primary drivers of community willingness. The evidence shows that community participation is influenced by a range of factors, not just financial incentives.

77.Option: The initial fear and suspicion toward tsetse traps in HAT− villages are primarily due to the traps' appearance and color, rather than cultural or supernatural associations.
Evidence: The suspicions and fear were not reported to be in any way related to the appearance of traps, but rather its position next to the river, uncertainty about the person who carried out the deployment and the unknown purpose of this action, as one of the participants explained: 'Since it's just a cloth, there would not be much fear… The fear would be: who has placed it there! For what [purpose]'.

The strong emotional reactions expressed by both groups during the first contact with traps were related to the associations of traps with supernatural powers. Participants explained, for instance, that unusual objects, activities or 'suspicious' people moving in their environment made them apprehensive.
77.Rationale: The passage indicates that the initial fear and suspicion toward tsetse traps in HAT− villages are primarily due to cultural and supernatural associations, not the appearance and color of the traps. The evidence shows that the fear and suspicion are related to the unknown purpose of the traps and their association with supernatural powers, rather than their physical appearance.

78.Option: The persistence of clinical signs of trachoma after the clearance of C. trachomatis infection indicates that the current WHO guidelines for trachoma elimination are ineffective in preventing long-term ocular damage.
Evidence: The passage does not provide any information about the persistence of clinical signs of trachoma after the clearance of C. trachomatis infection, nor does it comment on the effectiveness of the current WHO guidelines in preventing long-term ocular damage.
78.Rationale: The passage does not provide any evidence to support the claim that the WHO guidelines are ineffective in preventing long-term ocular damage. The passage only discusses the discrepancy between clinical signs and infection status, and the challenges in determining when to stop treatment in low prevalence settings. It does not make any claims about the effectiveness of the WHO guidelines.

78.Option: The lack of a control group in the study design means that the observed decline in C. trachomatis infection cannot be attributed to mass drug administration, and therefore antibiotic treatment should not be used in low prevalence trachoma settings.
Evidence: The passage does not mention anything about the lack of a control group in the study design, nor does it suggest that antibiotic treatment should not be used in low prevalence trachoma settings.
78.Rationale: The passage does not provide any information about the study design or the use of a control group. It also does not suggest that antibiotic treatment should not be used in low prevalence trachoma settings. The passage only discusses the challenges in determining when to stop treatment in low prevalence settings.

78.Option: The observed re-infection episode in two villages shortly after mass antibiotic treatment was associated with travel to a religious festival in Senegal, suggesting that large-scale community gatherings may act as critical hubs for the reintroduction of C. trachomatis in low prevalence settings.
Evidence: In contrast, in villages 1 and 3 the prevalence of infection increased at two months (Table 5). This increase was strongly associated with the travel of most village residents to a religious festival in Senegal one month after receiving treatment, which has been previously reported (OR 12.2, p<0.0001, 95% CI 4.0–44.1) [9]. After the 2-month assessment, infection prevalence in these two villages declined progressively to zero at five years (Table 5).

The risk of infection at any follow-up time point did not differ between those who had received treatment at baseline and those who had not or who were new residents during the follow-up (data not shown).
78.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of a re-infection episode in two villages shortly after mass antibiotic treatment, which was associated with travel to a religious festival in Senegal. This suggests that large-scale community gatherings may act as critical hubs for the reintroduction of C. trachomatis in low prevalence settings.

78.Option: The marked reduction in fly-eye contacts and unclean faces observed during the study suggests that health education on facial cleanliness was the primary driver of the decline in trachoma prevalence, independent of antibiotic treatment.
Evidence: The passage does not provide any evidence to support the claim that health education on facial cleanliness was the primary driver of the decline in trachoma prevalence, independent of antibiotic treatment.
78.Rationale: The passage does not provide any evidence to support the claim that health education on facial cleanliness was the primary driver of the decline in trachoma prevalence. It only mentions that there was a marked reduction in fly-eye contacts and unclean faces, but does not attribute this to health education.

78.Option: Applying the current WHO intervention algorithms in low prevalence trachoma settings may lead to unnecessary rounds of mass drug administration in uninfected communities.
Evidence: Unfortunately, there is often a mismatch between disease and infection: infection may detected without clinical disease and disease may be found without infection. The correlation between disease and infection is not particularly strong and tends to weaken as the community prevalence drops [5], [6]. This led the WHO to recommend the use of mass drug administration (MDA) with either single –dose oral azithromycin (which has therapeutic tissue concentrations for several days) or topical tetracycline to entire communities or districts [7].

The WHO also published specific Ultimate Intervention Goals (UIG); targets that need to be reached for the national certification of elimination of blinding trachoma [8]. For the surgery component of the SAFE strategy the UIG is to bring the population prevalence of TT below 0.1% in people aged 15 years and over. For the “AFE” components the UIG is to bring TF%1–9 below 5%. At this level it is expected that C. trachomatis infection will be too infrequent to drive scarring, although, there are no published data demonstrating this.
78.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the correlation between disease and infection is not particularly strong and tends to weaken as the community prevalence drops. Evidence: Here we investigated whether the FP assay could be applied to a more complex, multicellular species such as a nematode worm. To investigate this possibility, we prepared extracts from B. pahangi and from the GA-insensitive nematode C. elegans, and probed the affinity of the fluorescent GA molecule, cy3B-GA, for the Hsp90 species characteristic of these two nematodes (Figure 1).

The assay results concord well with previous observations that despite a high degree of conservation between the two nematode Hsp90 sequences [20] and an essentially overlapping binding mode predicted computationally (Figure 1D), GA has no significant effect on C. elegans [19].

To investigate whether the results of the FP assay correlate with the effects of Hsp90 inhibitors on Brugia viability and Mf output, the effect of two PU-scaffold compounds on worm viability was assessed. Adult female worms of B. pahangi were incubated in medium containing either GA at 1 µM [14], or the two synthetic inhibitors PU-H71 and PU-DZ8 at 5 and 10 µM. All agents had a profound effect on Mf release (Figure 3D), with a trend in potency that followed their affinity for Brugia Hsp90 (GA > PU-H71 > PU-DZ8 see Figure 3C).

To evaluate whether the assay is sufficiently sensitive to differentiate between human and Brugia Hsp90 species, we measured the affinity of GA and of several closely related PU-class derivatives, PU-24FCl, PU-H71 and PU-WS10 for the Brugia (Figure 4A) and the SKBr3 (Figure 4B) Hsp90 complexes. While GA interacted with identical affinity with both human and Brugia species, a good selectivity was noted in the PU-series, with a selectivity ratio of 2, 3.5 and 9 for PU-24FCl, PU-H71 and PU-WS10, respectively (Figure 4C).

Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the FP assay, originally developed for tumor cell Hsp90, is applicable to Brugia and can detect specific and dose-dependent inhibition of cy3B-GA binding by known Hsp90 inhibitors. It also shows that the assay can distinguish between Brugia and human Hsp90 species, indicating its utility in drug discovery for parasitic nematodes. Therefore, this option is correct.

This finding was observed in all T. cruzi strains assayed, belonging to different lineages, and proved that TcTASV-C is upregulated in bloodstream forms.

This inference is based on the evidence of its widespread expression and the fact that it is not detected in the same levels in culture-derived trypomastigotes, implying a role in the parasite's life cycle and interaction with the host's immune system.

119.Option: Malathion selection had a negative effect on resistance to other insecticides, including temephos and deltamethrin, suggesting that resistance mechanisms to malathion may divert metabolic resources away from resistance to other classes of insecticides.
Evidence: Selection with malathion did not induce cross-resistance with temephos, although both are OP (Tables 1 and S3). In contrast, temephos resistance decrease was more noticeable in malathion selected groups than in the control ones–both RR and SR indexes suggested such a trend. In particular, temephos resistance reduction was more pronounced in Crato, the population with the highest initial RR.

When the present study started, diflubenzuron had been recently introduced in the country to control A. aegypti larvae. Resistance indexes for this CSI compares adult emergency inhibition rates (EI), and not lethal concentrations. When compared to the original populations, although some differences in RR and SR for DFB were observed between C and S groups, all values obtained were considered compatible with a susceptible status (Tables 1 and S4).

All samples were considered highly resistant to deltamethrin. Surprisingly, the simple laboratory rearing, in the absence of any insecticide (groups C1, C2), exacerbated PY resistance levels in both populations (Tables 1 and S5). In particular, the SR index confirmed the marked deltamethrin resistance increase in the control groups, mainly in Aracaju (S5 Table). This increase in deltamethrin resistance, however, was not observed in the S1-3 groups, selected with malathion, independently of the analytical method adopted (RR, SR and 95% confidence limits overlapping). In opposition, malathion selected groups, and particularly Crato mosquitoes, exhibited reduction of deltamethrin resistance levels.
119.Rationale: The passage indicates that malathion selection led to a decrease in resistance to temephos and deltamethrin, which are different classes of insecticides. This suggests that the resistance mechanisms to malathion may be different from those to other insecticides, potentially diverting metabolic resources and affecting the resistance to other insecticides. The evidence shows that malathion selection reduced the resistance to these insecticides, which supports the inference that resistance mechanisms to malathion may impact resistance to other insecticides.

119.Option: The lack of changes in acetylcholinesterase (Ace) activity suggests that malathion resistance in A. aegypti is primarily mediated by metabolic detoxification rather than target-site insensitivity.
Evidence: Total activity of the OP target site, AChE, remained equivalent to the original samples. We also investigated Ace using the WHO susceptibility criterion: inhibition of 70% or more of Ace's activity with propoxur (AChI); in all cases the results are indicative of a susceptible enzyme (Tables 3, 4 and S7). According to this criterion, Ace of both parental Aracaju and Crato populations has a susceptible profile.
119.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the acetylcholinesterase (Ace) activity remained equivalent to the original samples and that the results are indicative of a susceptible enzyme. This directly contradicts the inference that malathion resistance in A. aegypti is primarily mediated by metabolic detoxification, as the evidence shows that Ace activity did not change, indicating that target-site insensitivity is not a primary resistance mechanism in this case.

119.Option: The higher metabolic resistance observed in adult mosquitoes compared to larvae suggests that adult-stage selection would be more effective in generating cross-resistance to other insecticides.
Evidence: In general, no differences were observed between control groups and those exposed to malathion which could be classified as diagnostic of the selected resistance.

In relation to the parental samples, Aracaju adult females tended to present higher MFO and GST activities, the later being revealed by both criteria in control samples (Tables 3 and S7) but detected only after comparison of median values in S ones (S7 Table). In Crato, marked alterations of all classes of enzymes were also observed in both C and S females, when compared to the Parental population; however, GST alterations were more attenuated in relation to the other enzyme classes, being detected only by medians comparison (Tables 4 and S7).

In the larval stage, no metabolic alterations were detected in Aracaju C and S samples, compared to the original population. In Crato larvae, α-EST EST activity was consistently enhanced and MFO alterations were noted in some samples when median values were compared, particularly in the malathion selected ones.
119.Rationale: The passage does not provide a direct comparison of metabolic resistance between adult and larval stages. It only mentions that adult mosquitoes tend to have higher MFO and GST activities, and that there are no metabolic alterations in the larval stage for Aracaju samples. It also notes that in Crato larvae, α-EST EST activity was consistently enhanced. There is no clear evidence to support the inference that adult-stage selection would be more effective in generating cross-resistance to other insecticides.

119.Option: The reduction in deltamethrin resistance in malathion-selected populations indicates that malathion and deltamethrin share a common resistance mechanism, leading to competitive inhibition.

In particular, the SR index confirmed the marked deltamethrin resistance increase in the control groups, mainly in Aracaju (S5 Table). This increase in deltamethrin resistance, however, was not observed in the S1-3 groups, selected with malathion, independently of the analytical method adopted (RR, SR and 95% confidence limits overlapping). In opposition, malathion selected groups, and particularly Crato mosquitoes, exhibited reduction of deltamethrin resistance levels.

When compared to the parental samples and as judged by slope values, homogeneity of all evaluated groups remained unaltered or increased (S1–S5 Tables). Only with deltamethrin bioassays there was a trend towards increased heterogeneity (except for Crato S groups). In this regard, the unusual and specific increase in deltamethrin resistance, noted in the control groups, should be considered.
119.Rationale: The passage indicates that malathion selection led to a reduction in deltamethrin resistance, but it does not provide evidence that malathion and deltamethrin share a common resistance mechanism. The reduction in resistance could be due to other factors, such as the specific selection process or the absence of insecticide exposure. There is no direct evidence in the passage to support the inference of competitive inhibition between malathion and deltamethrin.

120.Option: The high correlation between pathogenic Leptospira species and large agricultural animals such as cattle and water buffalo in Kandy suggests that these animals are the primary source of environmental contamination in paddy fields, despite the potential for indirect associations due to resuspension of soil particles.
Evidence: In Sri Lanka, cattle demonstrate a high seropositive rate for leptospirosis.
Most small-holder dairy farmers allow their cattle to graze in paddy fields before the cultivation starts, thus, household animals seemed to be important for transmission of leptospirosis to humans.

A case-control study of environmental and occupational risks of leptospirosis in Sri Lanka also showed that paddy workers have a higher odds ratio of leptospirosis transmission than non-paddy workers.
In order to understand Leptospira transmission cycle, pathogenic Leptospira species from several environmental sources were successfully isolated using a novel combination of antimicrobial agents.

The identification of the pathogenic Leptospira reservoir animals in the different agro-ecological regions of Sri Lanka will be useful to understand the eco-epidemiology of leptospirosis and establish proper prevention and intervention strategies.
120.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of a high seropositive rate for leptospirosis in cattle and the practice of allowing cattle to graze in paddy fields, which suggests that these animals are a significant source of environmental contamination. The correlation between paddy workers and higher odds of transmission further supports the role of these animals in the transmission cycle. The study's aim to understand the transmission cycle and the identification of reservoir animals as a key to understanding the eco-epidemiology of leptospirosis also supports this inference. The potential for indirect associations due to resuspension of soil particles is acknowledged, but the primary source of contamination is still indicated to be the large agricultural animals.

121.Option: The use of vaccination cards as a verification method likely underestimates true coverage because many residents may have lost or not received them, leading to an overestimation of recall-based coverage.
Evidence: OCV coverage by respondents’ recall was 91% (95% CI: 88%‒93%) for the first dose and 85% (95% CI: 81%‒89%) for the second dose, among the 1,102 individuals aged ≥1 year in households surveyed at first follow-up.
Vaccination cards were available for 443 (40%) individuals.

Assuming that all 203 individuals in the 46 non-responding households were unvaccinated, OCV coverage in the camp overall could have been as low as 77% (95% CI: 72%‒82%) and 72% (95% CI: 67%‒77%) for the first and second dose, respectively.

This suggests that the recall-based coverage might be overestimated because it does not account for those who may have lost their cards or did not receive them. The coverage rates based on recall are therefore likely to be higher than the true coverage, as they do not account for these potential discrepancies.

158.Option: The induction of autophagy promotes T. cruzi metacyclogenesis, as evidenced by increased metacyclic trypomastigote formation and higher infection rates in Vero cells when autophagy is stimulated by rapamycin or spermidine.
Evidence: The percentage of MT differentiated up to 48 h was higher in both TAU medium at the first (TAU-F) and the total (TAU-T) period of metacyclogenesis than in controls (12.1 +/- 0.9% and 16.4 +/- 1.7% vs. 3.9 +/- 0.6%, Fig 6A). Furthermore the addition of Wort to TAU during the first (Wort-F) or the total (Wort-T) period significantly reduced the number of MT in the parasite mixture, as compared to TAU-T condition. Conversely, Rap was able to increase the generation of MT when added to control medium at the first period of differentiation (8.3 +/- 0.2% vs. 3.9 +/- 0.6%). In another set of experiments, samples were allowed to infect Vero cells monolayers during 24 h and then fixed and processed for confocal microscopy. The degree of cell infection was considered an indirect measure of the number of MT present in the samples at different conditions, as previously described [22]. As depicted in Fig 6B, parasite nuclei were visualized in green due to the stable expression of TcH2b histone fused to GFP (24), whereas host cells were in red due to the labeling with phalloidin-rhodamine that binds to actin cytoskeleton. The blue color depicts cell and parasite nuclei and parasite kinetoplast stained with Hoechst. Results showed that around 45% of cells were infected by TAU differentiated parasites, whereas 10% of the cells were infected by control parasites. The presence of Wort at either the first (Wort-F) or the whole (Wort-T) period of differentiation in TAU significantly reduced the infection rate. In concordance to the above result, the addition of Rap at the first period of time (Rap-F) induced a significant increase of infectivity (Fig 6C).

Taken together, these results demonstrated that autophagy has a key role during T. cruzi differentiation. Therefore, modulation of autophagy can be used to interrupt the metacyclogenesis rate in T. cruzi and to block the normal progression of the parasite biological cycle.

159.Option: The induction of both IFN-γ and IL-10 production in vaccinated mice suggests that the immune response is balanced and may be sufficient for long-term protection against visceral leishmaniasis.
Evidence: Given that production of IFN-γ is considered an important requirement for protection against L. infantum and that the presence of a small amount of IL-10 could promote the induction of type-1 immunity [64], we determined the levels of IFN-γ and IL-10 at different times pre- and post-challenge in cultures of splenocytes isolated from immunized and control mice in response to rA2-rCPA-rCPB, F/T L.tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE-EGFP and F/T L. infantum as recall antigens (Fig. 3A–C).
The production of IL-10 upon antigen stimulation at 4-week and especially at 8 weeks after challenge was lower in G1 and G2 vaccinated groups in comparison to the control groups (G3 to G5) (Fig. 3B).

We further calculated the IFN-γ to IL-10 ratio for each vaccinated group as a clear indicator of successful immunization [33] (Fig. 3C). Indeed, the Leishmania-specific IFN-γ/IL-10 ratios upon stimulation with rA2-rCPA-rCPB and L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE-GFP recall antigens were higher in G1 and G2 compared to the control G3, G4 and G5 mice both at 4 and 8 weeks after challenge. Moreover, a clear Spearman correlation was observed between the ratio of IFN-γ/IL-10 production and differences in parasite burden in liver (−.729*(p = .017)) and spleen cells (−.640*(p = .046)). This means that higher amount of IFN-γ/IL-10 ratio resulted in lower parasite burden in both spleen and liver at 4th week after challenge. The IFN-γ/IL-10 ratio was drawn against the parasite burden in both spleen and liver at 4th week after challenge with individual values of each group (five groups) in liver and spleen as well as their mean, and the negative slops in both graphs are in concordance with this fact (Fig. S3A and S3B).

The highest amount of nitric oxide, which is essential for killing parasites inside infected macrophages, was observed with the Live/Live modality vaccinated mice (G2) upon stimulation with rA2-rCPA-rCPB, F/T L.tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE-GFP and F/T L. infantum antigens at 8 weeks after challenge (Fig. 3E).
159.Rationale: The passage indicates that the production of IL-10 is lower in vaccinated groups compared to control groups, which suggests that the immune response is not balanced but rather skewed towards a type-1 response, which is characterized by high levels of IFN-γ and low levels of IL-10. This is important for protection against visceral leishmaniasis. The passage does not support the idea that the immune response is balanced, as a balanced response would likely involve higher levels of IL-10, which is not observed in the vaccinated groups.

Histopathological Studies in the Vaccinated Mice

G1 to G5 groups are as indicated in Figure 2. The number of independent repeats was two and number of mice per group/time point n = 2 and all tests were done in duplicate (number of mice per group/time point n = 2) and the results are pooled and shown as mean±S.E. of measures obtained from 4 mice of each group. The asterisk indicates the significant difference between values at the indicated time points as determined by Student's test (p<0.05 denoted as *, p<0.01 denoted as **, p<0.001 denoted as *** and n.s. denoted as non significant).

In the liver, mononuclear cell infiltration in portals was more prominent in non-vaccinated groups at week 14th. Plasma cells were only seen in these groups. Interface hepatitis was not a significant finding. Parasites in the portals were only seen in non-vaccinated groups (G3 and G5) in 8th week (data not shown).

In all groups, lobular inflammation was seen at 4th week, increased significantly in 8th week and decreased at week 14. The severity of this inflammatory response at 4th week was higher in G3 compared to the other groups (15–16/10 hpf (high power field) v.s 2–4/10 hpf of vaccinated groups, p<0.05) (Fig. 6). Inflammatory cell infiltration was seen in the liver parenchyma in groups 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 at 4th week after challenge (Fig. S4). Portal granuloma formation (either mature or immature) was seen as early as 8th week in non vaccinated group, but in parenchyma of all groups they were seen at this time and persisted until 14th week (Fig. 5C and 5D). Parasite in the parenchyma was visualized only in one out of 12 mice of vaccinated groups but it was seen in 11 out of 18 non-vaccinated mice. Occurrence of cholestasis was noted at 8th week with no predilection to any group (data not shown). There was no significant difference in neutrophil infiltration, fatty changes, giant cell formation, and kupffer cell hyperplasia between groups (data not shown). Liver cell necrosis was minimal and no significant regenerative changes were noted (data not shown). No parasites were found in bone marrow macrophages and no significant changes in bone marrow of all examined mice were observed (data not shown).

G1 to G5 groups are as indicated in Figure 2. The asterisk indicates the significant difference between values at the indicated time points as determined by Student's test (p<0.05 denoted as *, p<0.01 denoted as **, p<0.001 denoted as *** and n.s. denoted as non significant).

160.Option: The fact that both P. perniciosus and P. perfiliewi were more attracted to infected dogs in the field suggests that these sand fly species have evolved a specific preference for L. infantum-infected hosts as part of their natural feeding behavior.
Evidence: A total of 1,939 sand flies were collected during 27 trapping-nights. Phlebotomus perniciosus was the most abundant sand fly species (n = 1228, 63.3%) followed by P. perfiliewi (n = 662, 34.1%). The remaining sand fly species were Phlebotomus papatasi (n = 11, 0.56%), and Sergentomyia minuta (n = 38, 1.96%).

The numbers of P. perniciosus attracted to the infected dog, uninfected dog, and blank control were 20.44 ± 4.71, 46.78 ± 7.62, and 1.89 ± 0.63, respectively (Fig 7). Overall, there was a significant (ANOVA: F = 35.8120, DF = 2, P <0.001) difference in the numbers of P. perniciosus collected in the different cages. Infected dog attracted more P. perniciosus than uninfected dog (MCP: z = 3.666, P<0.001).

The numbers of P. perfiliewi attracted towards the infected dog, uninfected dog, and empty control cage were 27.22 ± 8.79, 9.39 ± 2.59 and 0.17 ± 0.09, respectively (Fig 9). Overall, the difference between numbers of P. perifiliewi collected in the cages with the infected dog, uninfected dog, and empty control cage was statistically significant (ANOVA: F = 94.715, DF = 2, P <0.001). Infected dog attracted more P. perfiliewi than uninfected dog (EMMEANS: z = 7.931, p<0.001).
Both infected and uninfected dogs attracted more P. perfiliewi than empty control cage (EMMEANS: infected: z = 13.440, P<0.001; uninfected: z = 9.005, P<0.001).
160.Rationale: The passage provides data showing that both P. perniciosus and P. perfiliewi were significantly more attracted to infected dogs compared to uninfected dogs in the field. This suggests that these sand fly species have a natural preference for feeding on hosts that are infected with L. infantum, which is the parasite causing visceral leishmaniasis. The data supports the inference that these sand flies have evolved a specific preference for L. infantum-infected hosts as part of their natural feeding behavior.

In view of the importance of the Mouhoun eradication project, and the limited number of samples (three) used in previous study [26], it was deemed necessary to expand these studies and to obtain more data on the dispersal potential of the two tsetse species present, as evidenced through genetic structures of the various populations.

Evidence: As can be seen from Table 5, there is a significant genetic signature of sex biased dispersal in G. tachinoides, with the female flies having a lower dispersal rate (male biased dispersal).

For G. p. gambiensis several sex biased dispersal tests were carried out: between sites over all river basins and between sites within the Mouhoun river basins, between traps within the Mouhoun river basin and between traps within sites. For the first and second tests, only one male and one female per trap were used, or only a single individual if only one sex was available, per trap and individuals of the same site considered as belonging to the same subpopulation. This data reduction was done to limit as much as possible the confounding effect of the significant differentiation that exists between traps in this species (see [51] for comments on that matter). A single test resulted in a significant P-value (Table 6), with the mAIc indicating a female biased dispersal. However, it can be seen from Table 6 that biased dispersal genetic signatures are inconsistent across parameters in the same analysis or across analyses for the same parameter. As previously observed [26], the most obvious conclusion, is that no genetic signature of sex biased dispersal could be detected in G. p. gambiensis at any level.

Evidence: It has been known that CFTR-mediated transepithelial Cl– secretion provides the driving force for CT-induced intestinal fluid secretion.
Pathogenesis of diarrhea in cholera required V. cholerae colonization and production of CT.

Possibility of paracellular leakage as a cause of V. cholerae-induced intestinal fluid accumulation was investigated using both ex vivo measurements of transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) of mouse intestine by Ussing chamber systems and in vivo FITC-dextran trans-intestinal flux assays. As shown in Fig. 4A, TEER of V. cholerae-inoculated intestinal tissues was not significantly different from that of saline-injected control and CT-injected groups.

To determine the contribution of vascular leakage to V. cholerae-induced intestinal fluid accumulation in this model, the amount of the plasma protein albumin was determined in fecal fluids of both control (no V. cholerae) and V. cholerae-inoculated mice. It was found that levels of albumin in fecal fluid of both control and V. cholerae-inoculated intestinal loops were under detection limit (<0.3 g/dL).
These results suggested that paracellular permeability of the mouse intestine was unaltered by V. cholerae infection and CT exposure.

The BIC of the full model is 1214.08 and the BIC of the null model is 1467.84. The residual diagnostic plot (S1 File), does not show any nonlinear trend and non-constant variance. The plot is also symmetric with respect to 0, thus we did not find any issues in the model fitting.

Holding other variables constant, we found that JE fatality risk decreased over time (OR: 0.965; 95% CI: 0.947–0.983) (Table 2). Younger JE cases were associated with higher JE fatality risk (OR: 1.012; 95% CI: 1.003–1.021). JE fatality risk odds in JE immunized countries is 0.802 (90% CI: 0.653–0.994; 95% CI: 0.62–1.033) times of odds in JE unimmunized countries (Table 2). Ten percentage increase in the percentage of rural population to the total population was associated with 15.35% (95% CI: 7.71, 22.57) decrease in JE fatality odds. Ten percentage increase in population growth rate is correlated with 3.71% (90% CI: 0.23, 7.18; 95% CI: -0.4, 8.15) increase in JE fatality odds (Table 2). Adjusting for the effect of year, rural population percent, age of JE cases, and population growth rate, the likelihood of JE fatality risk in India is higher than in China. (OR: 5.46, 95% CI: 3.61–8.31) (Table 2).

In sensitivity analysis, we ran the JE inference model without JE records from China and India one at a time and in both cases we observe that lowest Age of JE patients and the population growth (in %) were no longer significant at 5% (S2 Table).
214.Rationale: The passage indicates that the fatality risk decreased over time, which could be associated with improved case confirmation methods and the use of the WHO case definition in surveillance systems. The inference that the reduced JE CFR estimate after 2000 is due to these factors is supported by the evidence that the fatality risk decreased over time and that the model's performance did not change significantly when data from China and India were excluded, suggesting that these factors are important in the model.
